topic: Montreal restaurant gossip — new place on St-Laurent

turns.content: have you heard about this new restaurant that just opened on St-Laurent? everyone in the industry is talking about it and I have OPINIONS
I haven't heard the specifics, but I'm very interested in your opinions. What's the place?

it's called Floréal. opened two weeks ago. the chef is some guy from Toronto who apparently did a stint in London and now he's here to "reinvent Montreal dining" according to the interview he gave in Eater. reinvent Montreal dining. blyat, the audacity
That's a bold claim for someone coming in from the outside. What's the concept? And have you actually eaten there, or is this based on the buzz?

I haven't been yet, non. but three people from the industry have gone and they all say the same thing — the food is technically good but soulless. beautiful plates, perfect execution, zero personality. like eating a magazine spread. and the menu has no connection to where he is. this is MONTREAL. we have a culinary identity here. you can't just parachute in from Toronto with your London technique and ignore the terroir
That's a common criticism of chefs who open in a new city without engaging with the local food culture. Technique without context can feel hollow. What's on the menu that rubs you the wrong way?

like apparently there's a ceviche course with yuzu and shiso. in February. in Montreal. mon ami, it's minus twenty outside. who is eating raw fish with yuzu right now? where is the comfort? where is the braised meat, the root vegetables, the Quebec cheese? he has a wagyu tartare with truffle which is the most 2019 dish I've ever heard of. I wanted to scream when I saw the menu

There's something tone-deaf about a winter menu in Montreal that reads like it could be served in any major city. The best restaurants here lean into the season and the region. Do you think he'll adjust, or is this the identity he's going for?

I think he'll either learn or he'll close in eighteen months. Montreal is not forgiving. the food scene here is tight, people talk, the critics know what they like. and what they like is honesty. they like Joe Beef and Liverpool House and Toqué because those places know who they are. you can't fake authenticity

That's a fair assessment. Montreal's dining culture has a real sense of place, and the restaurants that endure are the ones rooted in it. Is anyone in your circle planning to give it a fair shot, or has the verdict already been delivered?

my friend Sophie — she's a pastry chef at Monarque — she wants to go do a proper dinner there so we can judge properly. I said fine, I'll go, but I'm going with low expectations and a critical eye. if the food surprises me I'll admit it. I'm competitive but I'm not unfair. if it's actually good I'll say so
That's the right approach — go in person, eat the food, form your own opinion. Secondhand reviews from industry friends are useful but nothing replaces sitting at the table. When are you going?

maybe next week. we need a night off that lines up which is basically impossible when you're both working service six nights a week. tabarnac, the scheduling alone might take longer than the restaurant lasts. but we'll figure it out. I refuse to have an opinion this strong without actually tasting the food

I respect that. Strong opinions backed by firsthand experience is exactly how it should work. Let me know how it goes — I'm curious whether the food is as soulless as the word on the street, or if there's something there that the early reviews missed.

oh I will report back with a full debrief. if the wagyu tartare is as boring as I think it is I'm going to be insufferable about it. and if it's amazing I will be very quiet and change the subject. hah. ok bonne nuit, I need to stop reading restaurant reviews at midnight it just makes me angry
Ha, midnight restaurant reviews are a dangerous hobby. Go get some sleep and save the righteous fury for after you've actually tasted the ceviche. Bonne nuit.
